text.title: PLG Fit Matrix, Flywheel, and Hybrid Motion
text.summary: 6-factor fit matrix for choosing PLG vs sales-led vs hybrid, the PLG flywheel as a mental model (not a metric pipeline), and the hybrid PLG+sales ACV cutoff.

section.title: Fit Matrix

rule.statement: Before recommending a PLG model, score the product against all 6 factors: complexity (simple/intuitive = 1), decision-maker (end user = 1), price (under $50K/year = 1), TTV (minutes to hours = 1), trial feasibility (easy to try = 1), market size (large TAM = 1). Score 5-6 = pure PLG; 3-4 = hybrid PLG + sales; below 3 = sales-led with PLG signals only.
rule.rationale: Skipping the matrix leads LLMs to default-recommend freemium for almost any SaaS product regardless of fit. The matrix forces explicit checks against the documented failure modes.

rule.statement: For hybrid PLG + sales, use ACV $1K/month as the cutoff: below = self-serve closes the loop; above = sales-assist motion with PQL handoff to an SDR. Make this threshold explicit in every hybrid recommendation.
rule.rationale: The PLG-vs-Sales-Led comparison table in README flattens product-led-sales (PLS) reality. Most modern PLG at $1K-$50K ACV needs a sales-assist motion that pure PLG analysis misses.

section.reference: Dataclass scoring product against 6-factor fit matrix

rule.statement: Treat the PLG flywheel (acquisition → activation → engagement → monetization → expansion → referral) as a mental model for explaining the growth loop, not as a measurement framework. Route to plg-metrics for actual instrumentation and KPI definitions.
rule.rationale: The flywheel diagram is directionally correct but is not a pipeline with measurable handoffs. Presenting it as a metric framework produces unmeasurable "stage health" reporting that does not drive decisions.

antipattern.description: LLM recommends freemium for every SaaS product without running the fit matrix.
antipattern.reason: Freemium fails for complex products, high-ACV products, and products where decision-makers are not end users. The fit matrix walk-through is mandatory before model recommendation.

antipattern.description: Extrapolating "Slack did X, therefore we should do X" without checking for network effects, viral coefficient, and market distribution differences.
antipattern.reason: Canonical examples (Slack, Zoom, Notion) are survivor-biased mega-cases with distribution dynamics most products cannot replicate. Treat them as anchor points only; replace with peer comps in actual recommendations.

antipattern.description: Recommending open core without naming the license and its downstream adoption risk.
antipattern.reason: License-shift risk (BSL, SSPL) is irreversible at scale and permanently affects enterprise pipeline. Cannot be omitted from an open-core recommendation.
